steps.title: Identify the opening em-dash and what comes after
steps.content: The em-dash after “photographs” opens an explanation of what was photographed: “both of interviewees and the items they described.” After the comma introduces the specification of items (from hair to grass to sculptures), we reach “to powerful effect in her exhibition”—a phrase that describes the overall effect of Calle's pairing action.

steps.title: Choose punctuation that frames the parenthetical content
steps.content:
The comma after “described” correctly introduces the elaborating phrase “from hair to grass to sculptures” as a specification of which items.

An em-dash after “sculptures” creates the closing punctuation for the opening em-dash after “photographs,” framing the entire explanation as a parenthetical unit.

This em-dash also creates a strong boundary before “to powerful effect,” signaling that this final modifier stands apart from the list.

Option A lacks this closing boundary, Option C mismatches opening and closing punctuation (em-dash then comma), and Option D uses a colon—which introduces a list but doesn't address the parenthetical structure the opening em-dash requires.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
